entry.title: Building a Hybrid Landscape: Decentralized SAP EWM with S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Overview
Integrating SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) with SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition) brings advanced warehouse operations and core ERP processes into real-time alignment. In a decentralized deployment—where EWM runs on a separate system—interfaces must be configured for master data replication and transaction exchange so that inventory movements, deliveries and warehouse tasks remain in sync with S/4HANA Cloud. SAP fully supports this scenario using modern, cloud-friendly integration tools. This guide presents the available integration approaches, an ideal architecture, step-by-step instructions and best practices, including security considerations and prerequisites.

Supported Integration Methods & Technologies

  1. SAP BTP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration)
    • Serves as the central iPaaS for connecting S/4HANA Cloud to external systems.
    • Provides pre‑built connectors, mapping tools and standard integration packages to accelerate setup.
  2. Whitelisted S/4HANA Cloud APIs (OData, SOAP)
    • Only RESTful OData and SOAP services (including SOAP‑wrapped IDoc structures) are available for integration.
    • Standard APIs cover delivery creation, goods movement confirmation and other warehouse processes, enabling synchronous, real‑time communication between systems.
  3. IDoc Message Exchange via Middleware
    • Traditional ALE/IDoc formats (e.g., MATMAS, DEBMAS) are sent through the Integration Suite rather than direct RFC.
    • Provides near‑real‑time replication of master data and transactional messages.
  4. Event‑Driven Integration with SAP Event Mesh
    • S/4HANA Cloud publishes business events (e.g., "Outbound Delivery Created") to Event Mesh.
    • An event‑based approach triggers integration flows instantly, reducing polling and enabling decoupled, asynchronous updates.

Recommended Architecture & Components

  • Hub‑and‑Spoke Model: SAP Integration Suite acts as the central hub, routing data between S/4HANA Cloud and EWM.
  • Communication Arrangements: Defined in S/4HANA Cloud to specify which APIs or events are used and the target endpoints on BTP.
  • Adapters & Cloud Connector: Use HTTP(S)/SOAP adapters for S/4 calls and IDoc/RFC adapters for EWM, secured via the SAP Cloud Connector when EWM resides on-premises.
  • Pre‑Built Content: Leverage SAP’s integration packages from the API Business Hub or Best Practice Explorer where available.
  • Secure Connectivity: All communication over encrypted HTTPS, with OAuth or certificate‑based authentication and minimal firewall exposure.

Integration Process – High-Level Steps

  1. Activate Scope & Prepare Systems
    • Confirm relevant EWM integration scope items are included in the S/4HANA Cloud project (e.g., outbound, inbound, STO, physical inventory).
    • Ensure EWM runs in decentralized mode on a compatible release and basic warehouse structure is configured.
  2. Set Up Connectivity & Middleware
    • Install and configure SAP Cloud Connector for secure access to on‑prem EWM.
    • Provision Integration Suite tenant, create destinations, import or build iFlows for each scenario.
    • Configure Communication Systems and Arrangements in S/4HANA Cloud for each API or event.
  3. Master Data Replication
    • Synchronize materials, batches and business partners via IDoc or OData APIs through the Integration Suite.
    • Perform initial data loads to ensure EWM has up‑to‑date master records before transactions flow.
  4. Transactional Integration
    • Outbound: Sales order → outbound delivery → delivery distribution → EWM picks/ships → goods issue confirmation back to ERP.
    • Inbound: Purchase order → inbound delivery → EWM receives/putaway → goods receipt confirmation to ERP.
    • STO & Production: Similar flows for stock transport orders and production staging using delivery documents.
    • Physical Inventory: Bin‑level counts in EWM posting adjustments back to S/4 inventory.
  5. Monitoring & Error Handling
    • Use SAP Integration Suite monitoring plus AIF/WE05 in EWM and message monitoring in S/4HANA Cloud.
    • Configure retries, alerts and duplicate detection to ensure robust, idempotent processing.

Best Practices & Security

  • Use SAP Standard Content: Start with delivered APIs and integration packages to minimize custom code and ensure supportability.
  • Real‑Time Triggers: Leverage event or change‑based triggers instead of batch replication for critical transactions.
  • Data Governance: Enforce a policy that all master data originates in S/4HANA Cloud and is replicated to EWM before use.
  • Authentication & Network Security: Employ OAuth 2.0 or certificates, restrict Cloud Connector paths and encrypt all traffic.
  • Performance Considerations: Balance synchronous calls and asynchronous flows to optimize throughput while maintaining near real‑time sync.

Prerequisites & Limitations

Prerequisites:

  • SAP Integration Suite license and S/4HANA Cloud warehouse integration option.
  • Compatible EWM version (e.g., EWM 9.5 or S/4HANA 2022+ in decentralized mode).
  • Cloud Connector installed and configured, master data aligned.

Limitations:

  • Public Cloud restricts customization—no direct ABAP enhancements.
  • Some advanced EWM features may lack out‑of‑the‑box integration and require custom extensions.
  • Decentralized EWM requires duplicate master data maintenance and may introduce latency at scale.

References

  • SAP Help Portal – Basic Integration with SAP Extended Warehouse Management (SAP EWM): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/d73c28020d6046c7805db9490d72f41f.html
  • Integrating SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) On-Premise:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/implement-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-for-warehouse-management/integrating-sap-extended-warehouse-management-ewm-on-premise-inbound-processes_d2fc88c6-819d-4f55-bfce-78e66e0fae80

  • SAP Help Portal – What’s New in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 1805 (Supply Chain Integration Scope): https://help.sap.com/viewer/product/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/1805/en-US
  • SAP API Business Hub – Integration Packages and API Definitions: https://api.sap.com/

entry.title: SAP EWM Voice Picking Setup: Boost Accuracy and Productivity in Your Warehouse
entry.content:
Introduction

As warehouses evolve toward intelligent, agile operations, Pick-by-Voice (PBV) emerges as a standout innovation—enhancing picking speed, accuracy, and ergonomics. Integrated with SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), this hands-free solution transforms the way warehouse operators interact with systems.

In this blog, we’ll walk through what Pick-by-Voice offers, its business value, technical architecture, and a step-by-step configuration guide based on project experience. Whether you’re a consultant implementing EWM or a business leader driving digital transformation, this guide covers the essentials.

What Is Pick-by-Voice in SAP EWM?

Pick-by-Voice is a voice-directed picking solution that enables warehouse operators to receive and confirm instructions using voice input via headsets and wearable devices—eliminating the need to look at screens or use handheld scanners.

✅ Example: The system says, "Pick 8 from Bin B01" → Worker responds, "8 picked"

This method not only increases operator speed and focus but also minimizes fatigue and errors, making it a powerful ergonomic upgrade in warehouse environments.

Key Benefits for Businesses

Benefit

Impact

Increased Speed

Hands-free operation enables faster movements

Higher Accuracy

Voice confirmations reduce mispicks

Better Ergonomics

Eliminates the need to hold or look at devices

Quick Onboarding

New workers can be trained faster using voice-guided flows

Peak Readiness

Easier to scale during high-demand periods without needing extra devices

How Does SAP EWM Support Pick-by-Voice?

SAP EWM supports voice picking through its RF (Radio Frequency) Framework. It uses standard EWM transactions rendered as web pages using ITS Mobile (Internet Transaction Server). These pages are interpreted and vocalized by voice middleware (e.g., Honeywell Vocollect, Lydia Voice).

Important: SAP controls the logic and grammar but does not handle voice recognition or synthesis. Middleware handles the actual voice interface.

High-Level Architecture

 

  • SAP EWM provides standard RF transactions like /SCWM/RFUI and /SCWM/RFUI_PBV
  • ITS Mobile renders transactions into HTML accessible via browsers or device interfaces
  • Middleware translates those screens into voice dialogues
  • Devices (usually wearable) are connected over warehouse Wi-Fi

Implementation Steps for Consultants

Here's a structured implementation guide:

  1. Configure RF Transactions in EWM
  • Use /SCWM/RFUI_PBV for voice-specific processes
  • Tailor flow logic for minimal screen dependency
  • Ensure system-guided picking is enabled
  1. Create and Publish Internet Services
  • Go to SE80 → Create Internet Service (e.g., /SCWM/RFUI_PBV)
  • Use SICF to activate ICF node under /sap/bc/gui/sap/its
  • Assign handler CL_HTTP_EXT_ITS
  • Add GUI config parameters (e.g., ~ITSMOBILE, ~TRANSACTION, ~THEME, etc.)
  1. Generate HTML Templates

HTML templates are necessary for each dynpro screen used in voice-enabled processes. Generate them from the following function groups:

  • /SCWM/RF_TMPL (e.g., dynpro 0011)
  • /SCWM/RF_SSCR (dynpro 0011, 0012)
  • /SCWM/RSRC_DYNPRO (dynpros 0005, 0011, 0012, 0013)
  • /SCWM/RF_PBV (dedicated to Pick-by-Voice)

Style to use: MOBILEXV – Mobile Device with Speech Input

  1. Set Up Devices and Resources
  • Use /SCWM/PRDVC to create a device profile (assign the voice display profile)
  • Use /SCWM/RSRC to create a resource and assign the device
  • Use /SCWM/USER to link the resource to a user profile (with default values)
  1. Middleware and Device Setup
  • Deploy voice middleware like Honeywell Vocollect or Lydia Voice
  • Install and configure voice clients on ruggedized terminals
  • Validate Wi-Fi coverage across the warehouse floor
  1. Testing and Go-Live
  • Test the ICF service via browser using SICF
  • Validate HTML rendering and middleware voice parsing
  • Train end users with speech adaptation sessions
  • Conduct volume tests in noisy environments

Best Practices & Considerations

Challenge

Solution

Voice accuracy in noisy areas

Use noise-canceling headsets

Language/accent issues

Customize voice training for local workforce

HTML template errors

Pre-generate only required dynpros to avoid dumps

Wi-Fi blind spots

Conduct a wireless site survey and install boosters if needed

Middleware costs

Evaluate vendor licensing models early in the project

Note: With S/4HANA 2021 and above, SAP introduces EWM_MOBGUI — reducing dependency on HTML templates.

Conclusion

Pick-by-Voice is not just about technology — it's about empowering the warehouse workforce to be faster, safer, and more efficient. By integrating SAP EWM with voice-directed picking, businesses can dramatically reduce operational overhead while boosting productivity.

Whether you're designing the architecture or leading the transformation, now is the time to voice-enable your warehouse.

entry.title: Maximizing Value in Business Process Transformation: Advantages of Root Cause Analysis
entry.content:
In the race to transform businesses, why do so many organizations find themselves stuck at the starting line? The culprit might surprise you: it's not a lack of data, but an overabundance of it. Companies are drowning in process information yet starving for actionable insights. This "time-to-insight trap" has become the hidden bottleneck in business transformation efforts, silently eroding competitive advantage. 

But what if you could eliminate this trap entirely? Imagine compressing days of analysis into mere hours or even seconds, without sacrificing depth or accuracy. This is not an unattainable goal - it's the reality that Automated Root Cause Analysis (Automated RCA) is creating for forward-thinking organizations. 

In this article, we will explore how Automated RCA is empowering businesses like yours to 1) reduce time-to-insight by shifting the process transformation paradigm from analysis to action and 2) maximize value identified by surfacing key factors and quantifying them in monetary terms with value cases. 

Whether you're orchestrating large-scale transformations, optimizing operational processes, or seeking to instill a culture of continuous improvement, understanding the power of Automated RCA could be your key to an ROI-positive initiative. 

Understanding Why Your Metric Is Off Target Is Crucial 

Imagine you are a CPO with an ambitious goal: optimize your company's SAP-driven Source to Pay (S2P) process to boost the automation rate by 5% this quarter. Your SAP system is a goldmine of data, spanning the entire procurement lifecycle. You're equipped with the SAP Signavio Process Intelligence, including powerful tools like process mining and analysis dashboards. You know the potential for improvement is there, but fully leveraging these tools to their maximum potential can be challenging. 

While the SAP Signavio suite offers robust capabilities, some organizations find: 

  1. It takes significant time and expertise to identify the root causes for deviations between metric actuals and their corresponding targets. Are some purchasing organizations in conjunction with specific suppliers causing the 5% deviation? If so, what is the improvement potential? 
  2. Quantifying the monetary value of insights is key for ROI-positive initiatives. How may I translate improvement potential for the automation rate into monetary terms or conversely identify initiatives with which I may realize the business case? 
  3. Translating data-driven insights into actionable process improvements isn't always straightforward. What interventions should I make to lift the surfaced improvement potential? 

The result? Despite having powerful tools at your disposal, there's often a gap between generating insights and implementing meaningful changes in your S2P process. 

Value Identification — Automated Root Cause Analysis to the Rescue 

This is where Automated Root Cause Analysis enters the stage, by identifying root causes for target deviations 

  1. fully automatically, so that you can focus on interventions over analysis, 
  2. it surfaces improvement potential in seconds, not days or weeks, 
  3. and it prioritizes opportunities based on potential impact so that you can focus on what matters. 

Imagine you are analyzing your SAP-driven S2P process data. Instead of spending weeks manually sifting through complex procurement lifecycles, Automated RCA could instantly pinpoint that 80% of your automation shortfall is caused by specific purchasing organizations working with particular suppliers. 

But Automated RCA does not stop identifying problems. In conjunction with SAP Signavio's Value Cases, it translates insights into monetary terms, quantifying the value of each improvement opportunity. In our S2P example, it might show that addressing the identified issues could increase the automation rate by 4%, translating to a $1.5 million annual cost savings through reduced manual processing and improved efficiency. 

Value Realization — Turning Analysis into Action 

With a clear ROI at hand, Automated Root Cause Analysis helps to interpret the insights and guide you towards specific interventions to realize the surfaced improvement potential, moving from a purely diagnostic to a more action-oriented approach. 

For example, in our S2P automation scenario, the Automated RCA might not only identify problematic purchasing organizations and suppliers but also highlight areas where process changes could have the most significant impact. This could include focusing on specific supplier-buyer combinations or identifying bottlenecks in the current workflow that are hindering automation efforts. 

Each insight comes with projected impacts on automation rates and cost savings, providing you with a clear roadmap to achieve your 5% automation rate increase and realize the associated $1.5 million in annual cost savings. 

In conclusion, Automated Root Cause Analysis helps you, the customer, reach your KPIs more efficiently by eliminating the time-consuming guesswork of manual analysis. It pinpoints root causes, quantifies the value of improvements, and prioritizes areas with the highest impact potential. By accelerating your time-to-insight and turning data into clear, ROI-driven focus areas, Automated RCA ensures your transformation initiatives deliver measurable results—faster. 

Want to see more? Check out the click through demo! 

If interested, reach out, so we can show you what Automated RCA could bring you as a customer!

entry.title: Re: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 11 - Stay Future Ready with SAP EWM - Flexibility and Modularity
entry.content:
Hello Andreas,

All of your podcasts are excellent, and I always look forward to new episodes.

I returned to this podcast because of a related question.

In one scenario, #AGV s (automated guided vehicle) transport HUs (handling unit) from the GR area through the buffer area to the high racks

Two warehouse tasks are needed, since the movement of the HU from the GR (goods receipt) area to the buffer area is carried out by the AGV, while the movement from the buffer area to the high rack area is carried out by another resource (which can be an AGV or a forklift).

The buffer area has limited space and can hold only three HUs or in turn 3 bins.

When a truck delivers 30 HUs, 30 WTs (warehouse task) are automatically generated from the GR bins to the buffer bin, and an additional 30 WTs are created from the buffer bin to the high rack bins.

How do the AGVs work sequentially from the GR zone to the buffer zone? I mean, three WTs for three buffer bins are completed. Buffer bins are now full. Now the AGV should wait for the fourth WT until one of the buffer bins becomes available.

Is there a Badi? Is there any standard SAP EWM customizing that we can use? I am looking for #no-code #low-code customizing

thanks for the great blog and advice.

Manjushree

I’m unsure why this exchange rate is required, as I did not enter any payment information for the worker. The payment section in the import file is blank, so I assumed there would be no dependency on exchange rate data.

Could you please clarify why this error is occurring?

Best regards,

entry.title: Bandwidth Consumption Metric - Cloud integration
entry.content:
Introduction:

I often come across integrations that have not been designed to exploit the Message bandwidth optimally.
Before I expand any further on this, it would be good to familiarize yourself with the following blog.

https://www.integration-excellence.com/message-traffic-licensing-with-sap-integration-suite/

Below is an extract from the above blog.

What is a message in Cloud Integration?

  • In Cloud Integration each OUTGOING message from an Integration Flow is counted
    • E.g. if you have a splitter step within your iflow which sends 100 messages, you might see only 1 message in the monitor, but 100 are counted
  • Each message is counted up to 250 KB payload size, so when you transfer a 2 MB message, 9 messages will be charged (2 * 1024 / 250 = 8,192 => rounded to 9)
    • If you send each message into an archive (S3 bucket or whereever) it is also counted additionally
    • If you have a splitter step with 100 messages and 50 KB each within the same iflow message transfer (1 message in monitor), they are summed up and then the total is checked against the 250 KB size limit, so 20 messages will be charged (100 * 50 / 250 = 20)
  • Although only OUTGOING messages are counted, the payload from a reply of a request-reply step is also considered and charged
  • Retries are considered each time! Implement a circuit breaker for your iflows using JMS/Data Store by stopping the further processing after a configured amount of retries
  • Internal adapters, such as ProcessDirect and JMS are NOT considered as OUTGOING messages
  • Traffic via Edge Integration Cell is counted with a factor of 0.5 (50%) and there is only one runtime component (API Gateway and Cloud Integration Runtime is one technical runtime, thus a message is only counted once)

For now Let's talk about three types of Integrations

1) SAP To SAP - Prepackaged  / Standard Integrations

This category basically covers all pre packaged / out of the box integrations provided by SAP (configure and deploy). Outgoing Messages / Message Responses through these integrations are not chargeable and do not count towards the overall Bandwidth of your Cloud Integration Tenant.   

2) SAP to SAP - Modified / Custom Integrations

If you have built a custom integration / iflow on SAP CI or you have modified a prepackaged integration flow then the messages flowing through these IFlows will contribute towards the bandwidth of your tenant. If you have built an extension of a standard integration using post exits or pre exits then messages flowing through these exits / extensions will also count towards the bandwidth of your tenant.

3) SAP to Non SAP Integrations - Prepackaged / Custom

All outgoing messages for SAP to Non-SAP prepackaged / custom integrations are charged and count towards your tenants message bandwidth.

Now that we have cleared the basics (And someone correct me in comments if I am wrong about these assertions) lets move on to the next section.

Optimal Exploitation of the Bandwidth:

In good old days when systems were on-premise and we owned the hardware and were responsible for the network we didn't have to think twice about the number of messages flowing through the Integration platform as long as we were able to scale up the hardware and the network bandwidth if our overall outgoing increased.

Now that everything is on cloud and SAP Integration suite runtime is managed by SAP and the pricing is consumption based i.e. you pay for what you consume, we have to be really careful and  optimal while designing integrations. For example consider these scenarios

Scenario 1: Custom Integration: A Message hits SAP CI and has 50 records, SAP CI needs to do 4 lookups in SAP FSM and then based on some conditions based on the result of these lookups one record at a time will be updated in FSM through SAP CI with some additional fields probably which again come from these lookups.

This roughly translates to 250 outgoing messages of around 2-3 kb each in size.

What could have been improved? The above scenario could have been restricted to use 200 messages by doing a bulk insert into FSM if the API permitted bulk upload instead of doing 50 individual inserts.

Or the scenario could have been restricted to just 5 messages if all the api calls to FSM could have been batch calls

Before you get all riled up let's understand what is a Batch call nd what is a Bulk call?

Bulk operations typically involve processing a large number of records or operations in a single API call, often using a dedicated endpoint optimized for such large-scale actions (CREATE/UPDATE/POST). Batch operations, on the other hand, involve grouping multiple operations into a single request, which can be either a single API call or multiple calls within a larger context

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_FIELD_SERVICE_MANAGEMENT/fsm_bulk_api/bulk-api-overview.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_FIELD_SERVICE_MANAGEMENT/fsm_data_api/batch-request.html

Refer to above links to understand more.

Why is optimizing these integrations to use Message bandwidth optimally so important?

Depending on your license messages going out of SAP CI have a cost associated to them. The earlier you realize this and build you integration around this, the happier you would be. WHY?

Imagine discovering an integration is badly designed and is running in production and now needs to be redesigned to lets say reduce the over consumption by up-to 2-3 times. Great! you have a solution and you know how to optimize it but where is the ROI? Re-designing this will take some effort let's say 6000$'s and business will have to pay for it but it might just incur an additional few hundred dollars every month if it is allowed to run as is. Ultimately it's a loss for the business. Therefore design your integrations right the first time.  

Pro Tip: Make it a practice to save the outgoing message size in kilo bytes in a custom header for each outgoing message, i.e. just before the receiver adapter, insert a script that calculates the message size in kilobytes and adds that to a custom header. this will help you identify the problematic integrations.   

This brings us to the question, how to decide how optimal an integration is on a scale of 1 to 10 in terms of bandwidth.

In scenario 1 if the integration consumes 250 messages while it could have been designed to use just 5 messages, It would be a 0.2 on a scale of 1 to 100 , where 1 is the poorest and 100 is the most optimal state. 

How did we reach this?

( (number of messages it should optimally consume) / (total number of messages it consumes now) ) * 100

in our case (5 / 250) * 100 = 2. Basically on the lower side.

Conclusion

With SaaS tools like SAP FSM, Concur, SAP SF, its fairly common to do multiple lookups, inserts before sending out the final boss update for a SAP To SF integration for instance. This can result in exponential consumption of messages therefore its imperative that the bandwidth is used optimally.

entry.title: Re: BI launchpad Ajax request returning "403 - Access Den...
entry.content:
Resolution

In the nginx.conf file, in the server { tags, specify the below parameter:
underscores_in_headers on;

After doing this - restart nginx

 

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: ALV Grid editable cells
entry.content:
Editable ALV Report with Row-Based Selection Using LVC_ROWMARK
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* CLASS Event Handler for Double Click
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
CLASS LCL_EVENTS DEFINITION.
PUBLIC SECTION.
METHODS:on_edit_button for EVENT user_command of CL_GUI_ALV_GRID.
ENDCLASS.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* START-OF-SELECTION
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
START-OF-SELECTION.
data(obj_lcl) = new LCL_EVENTS( ).
CALL SCREEN 200.
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* SCREEN 200 - Header Screen
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE STATUS_0200 OUTPUT.
SET PF-STATUS 'ZSTANDARD'.
PERFORM LOAD_HEADER_DATA.
ENDMODULE.

MODULE USER_COMMAND_0200 INPUT.
CASE SY-UCOMM.
WHEN 'BACK'. LEAVE PROGRAM.
WHEN 'SAVE'.
PERFORM SAVE_HEADER_DATA.
WHEN 'EDIT'. " New custom function code for edit
OBJ_LCL->ON_EDIT_BUTTON( ).
ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

FORM LOAD_HEADER_DATA.
SELECT VBELN ERNAM ERDAT VSTEL VKORG
INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE GT_LIKP
FROM ZDB_EDIT_ALV
WHERE VBELN IN S_VBELN.

PERFORM INIT_CELL_STYLES CHANGING GT_LIKP.

PERFORM BUILD_FIELDCATALOG USING GT_FCAT1 'HEADER'.

IF O_CONT1 IS INITIAL.
CREATE OBJECT O_CONT1 EXPORTING CONTAINER_NAME = 'CONTAINER1'.
CREATE OBJECT O_GRID1 EXPORTING I_PARENT = O_CONT1.

GS_LAYOUT-STYLEFNAME = 'CELLTAB'.
GS_LAYOUT-SEL_MODE = 'D'.
GS_LAYOUT-NO_TOOLBAR = 'X'.

CALL METHOD O_GRID1->SET_TABLE_FOR_FIRST_DISPLAY
EXPORTING IS_LAYOUT = GS_LAYOUT
CHANGING IT_OUTTAB = GT_LIKP
IT_FIELDCATALOG = GT_FCAT1.

SET HANDLER OBJ_LCL->ON_EDIT_BUTTON FOR O_GRID1.

ELSE.
CALL METHOD O_GRID1->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY
EXPORTING IS_STABLE = VALUE LVC_S_STBL( ROW = 'X' COL = 'X' ).
ENDIF.
ENDFORM.

FORM SAVE_HEADER_DATA.
CALL METHOD O_GRID1->CHECK_CHANGED_DATA.
LOOP AT GT_LIKP INTO GS_LIKP.
UPDATE ZDB_EDIT_ALV SET
ERDAT = GS_LIKP-ERDAT
VSTEL = GS_LIKP-VSTEL
VKORG = GS_LIKP-VKORG
WHERE VBELN = GS_LIKP-VBELN AND ERNAM = GS_LIKP-ERNAM.
ENDLOOP.
COMMIT WORK.
MESSAGE 'Header data saved successfully' TYPE 'S'.
"Optional: Reset editable row
CLEAR GV_EDIT_ROW_INDEX.
ENDFORM.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Form: INIT_CELL_STYLES
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM INIT_CELL_STYLES CHANGING PT_TABLE TYPE STANDARD TABLE.
FIELD-SYMBOLS: <ROW> TYPE TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV.

DATA: LT_DISABLED_FIELDS TYPE LVC_T_STYL,
LS_STYLE TYPE LVC_S_STYL.

CLEAR LT_DISABLED_FIELDS.

INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'VBELN' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_DISABLED_FIELDS.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_DISABLED_FIELDS.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'ERDAT' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_DISABLED_FIELDS.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'VSTEL' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_DISABLED_FIELDS.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'VKORG' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_DISABLED_FIELDS.

LOOP AT PT_TABLE ASSIGNING <ROW>.
<ROW>-CELLTAB = LT_DISABLED_FIELDS.
ENDLOOP.
ENDFORM.

FORM BUILD_FIELDCATALOG USING PT_FCAT TYPE LVC_T_FCAT P_TYPE.
DATA: LS_FCAT TYPE LVC_S_FCAT.

CLEAR PT_FCAT.

IF P_TYPE = 'HEADER'.
CLEAR LS_FCAT.

LS_FCAT-FIELDNAME = 'VBELN'. LS_FCAT-COLTEXT = 'Delivery'. "LS_FCAT-MARK = 'X' .
APPEND LS_FCAT TO PT_FCAT.

LS_FCAT-FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM'. LS_FCAT-COLTEXT = 'Created By'. APPEND LS_FCAT TO PT_FCAT.

LS_FCAT-FIELDNAME = 'ERDAT'. LS_FCAT-COLTEXT = 'Created On'. LS_FCAT-EDIT = 'X'. APPEND LS_FCAT TO PT_FCAT.

LS_FCAT-FIELDNAME = 'VSTEL'. LS_FCAT-COLTEXT = 'Shipping Point'. LS_FCAT-EDIT = 'X'. APPEND LS_FCAT TO PT_FCAT.

LS_FCAT-FIELDNAME = 'VKORG'. LS_FCAT-COLTEXT = 'Sales Org'. LS_FCAT-EDIT = 'X'. APPEND LS_FCAT TO PT_FCAT.

ENDFORM.

CLASS LCL_EVENTS IMPLEMENTATION.

METHOD ON_EDIT_BUTTON.
FIELD-SYMBOLS: <LS_ROW> TYPE TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV.

DATA: LT_STYLE TYPE LVC_T_STYL,
LS_STYLE TYPE LVC_S_STYL,
LT_MARKED_ROWS TYPE LVC_T_ROW,
LS_ROW_MARK TYPE LVC_S_ROW,
lt_temp TYPE TABLE OF TY_ZDB_EDIT_ALV.

CLEAR: GV_EDIT_ROW_INDEX.

CALL METHOD O_GRID1->GET_SELECTED_ROWS
IMPORTING ET_INDEX_ROWS = LT_MARKED_ROWS.

READ TABLE LT_MARKED_ROWS INTO LS_ROW_MARK INDEX 1.
IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
GV_EDIT_ROW_INDEX = LS_ROW_MARK-INDEX.
ENDIF.
LT_TEMP = GT_LIKP.
LOOP AT LT_TEMP ASSIGNING <LS_ROW> FROM 1.
CLEAR LT_STYLE.

" Always disable primary keys
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'VBELN' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_STYLE.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'ERNAM' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_STYLE.

IF SY-TABIX = GV_EDIT_ROW_INDEX.
" Selected row – enable non-primary key fields
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'ERDAT' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_STYLE.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'VSTEL' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_STYLE.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'VKORG' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_ENABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_STYLE.
ELSE.
" Other rows – disable all fields
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'ERDAT' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_STYLE.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'VSTEL' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_STYLE.
INSERT VALUE #( FIELDNAME = 'VKORG' STYLE = CL_GUI_ALV_GRID=>MC_STYLE_DISABLED ) INTO TABLE LT_STYLE.
ENDIF.
<LS_ROW>-CELLTAB = LT_STYLE.
MODIFY GT_LIKP FROM <LS_ROW> INDEX SY-TABIX.
ENDLOOP.

O_GRID1->REFRESH_TABLE_DISPLAY( ).

CALL METHOD O_GRID1->SET_TABLE_FOR_FIRST_DISPLAY
EXPORTING IS_LAYOUT = GS_LAYOUT
CHANGING IT_OUTTAB = GT_LIKP
IT_FIELDCATALOG = GT_FCAT1.

GS_LAYOUT-STYLEFNAME = 'CELLTAB'.
GS_LAYOUT-SEL_MODE = 'D'.
GS_LAYOUT-NO_TOOLBAR = 'X'.

ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.
Editable ALV Report with Row-Based Selection Using LVC_ROWMARK
Description:
This ABAP report implements a two-screen ALV-based editable interface using CL_GUI_ALV_GRID. It features:
  • Display and editing of delivery header data from the custom table ZDB_EDIT_ALV.
  • Row-based selection using LVC_ROWMARK: Users can select a row and click the EDIT button to enable editing for non-primary key fields (ERDAT, VSTEL, VKORG) on the selected row only.
  • Primary key fields (VBELN, ERNAM) remain non-editable to ensure data integrity.
  • Editable cell behavior is controlled via the CELLTAB mechanism, dynamically applied based on user selection.
  • Toolbar function ‘EDIT’ is handled via a custom event method (ON_EDIT_BUTTON).
  • Data is updated in the database table upon clicking ‘SAVE’. Help me out this

entry.title: Re: BW: Parallel process not use BTC job of SM50 ?
entry.content:
Hi

The setting in the DTP is the maximum number of parallel processes that a DTP can start. Setting it to a certain number does not automatically mean it will start that many jobs. The start of additional child jobs (until the maximum is reached) is determined dynamically during runtime.

For example if the processing mode of the DTP is "Serial extraction, immediate parallel processing", then there will be 1 process, that does read the source serially and the rest will be processes, that process the transformation and the update step.

Let's say you have a DTP with processing mode "Serial Extraction, immediate parallel processing", that in total would create 200 data packages and you set the maximum number of parallel processes to 10. The expectation to start immediately 10 parallel processes is wrong.

If the extraction per data package takes longer, than the processing of those packages in the TRFN + Update step, then after 2-3 child jobs, no further processes are spawned.

If the processing for example within the TRFN is much higher, than it takes to extract the data, then after some time, that 10 process will be reached.

So it is a valid situation, that even you have defined 10 parallel processes for a DTP only 3 parallel processes are started.

Also if you have multiple ABAP application servers, it matters which host/group is set up in the DTP.

 

br

Roland

entry.title: Re: Ask about Exchange Rate Error in Manage Workforce
entry.content:
Hello dear user,

And thank you for asking your question in the SAP Community blog.

The error about the CZK/JPY exchange rate shows up because, even though you didn’t enter any payment info, the system has implicit dependencies in the system logic meaning it still checks currency conversions based on things like the worker’s company code or org unit settings.

If those are linked to CZK or JPY, the system expects a valid exchange rate for that date and rate type.

So, it’s not about what you entered, but about missing exchange rates in the system for those currencies.

To fix it :
Go to the Manage Exchange Rates app and add the missing CZK to JPY exchange rate for the relevant date and rate type “M.” That should clear the error.

I hope this will be helpful.

Best regards,

Jeremy

entry.title: Best Practices for Handling Large Data Volumes in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Best Practices for Handling Large Data Volumes in SAP Datasphere

Lessons Learned in Modeling and Initial/Historical Load Strategies

 

Who Is This Article For?

This article is intended for SAP Datasphere technical consultants, particularly those with a background in SAP BW.

This post does not aim to replicate best practices already covered in official SAP documentation (e.g., https://www.sap.com/documents/2021/09/8a0fc7ca-f67d-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html), nor does it focus on SQL-level or database-level performance tuning.

Instead, it highlights practical lessons learned from real-world projects, specifically related to data modeling and strategies for managing initial and historical data loads in SAP Datasphere. These insights are particularly relevant when dealing with large-scale datasets and complex data integration scenarios, where architectural decisions can significantly impact system performance and maintainability.

Use Case

The goal is to develop several dashboards using SAP Datasphere as the data warehouse platform. These dashboards share common datasets and must handle billions of records. This paper is based on a real-world case study involving the management of 1TB of data.

Are You a BW Consultant?

Congratulations! But don’t be misled.

 

The reality is that many SAP Datasphere consultants today come from the SAP BW world (myself included). While we all understand that these are fundamentally different products, it’s easy to fall into habits that are valid in BW but incorrect in Datasphere.

 

SAP BW natively handles large data loads using “packet” extractions. That is, a load involving millions of records is processed sequentially. For BW consultants, managing an initial load of billions of records is generally not a major concern—aside from the time it may take.

SAP Datasphere, however, lacks this functionality. While remote tables and view persistences support partitioning (which enables sequential loading), and Replication Flows can handle large volumes, upper-layer persistences may fail if these constraints are not properly modeled. Without a solid upfront load management strategy, the project is likely to fail.

 

Key Considerations

Before starting the project, it is essential to verify the tenant configuration (CU per section), considering the data volumes involved.

The classic “out of memory” error is not solely dependent on the memory allocated to the space or the view design. Other tenant parameters are also critically important.

Given the large data volumes, tables must be partitioned if they exceed HANA’s record limits. Note that a table cannot be partitioned retroactively—it must be flushed first.

 The SAP Datasphere analyzer can provide hints about which views to persist, although it often recommends persisting the final layer, which may not be optimal for reuse or memory management. 

 

Acquisition Layer

Avoid using remote tables. Instead, use Replication Flows based on delta-enabled CDS views.

 

Harmonization Layer

For large data volumes, view persistence is a weak strategy. Although views can be partitioned and partition-locked, this is not a sufficiently robust mechanism for handling large volumes with extensive history.

Persisting views complicates data temperature management, historical tracking, and more.

Instead of persisting views, use a Data Flow (DF) to load the view into a table.

Consider a “load by month” strategy (or similar depending of your needs). For example, use a table or view that determines the month to load, and have your views join with that table/view.

 

Aim to persist this layer rather than the upper ones, and create reusable persisted views/tables.

 

Propagation Layer

This layer should join with the harmonization views. If only joins are involved and the harmonization layer is already persisted, persisting this layer is unnecessary.

For handling temporalities, use the “Cartesian table” strategy (see SAP documentation https://www.sap.com/documents/2021/09/8a0fc7ca-f67d-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html )

In general, constrained ratios should be implemented in the analytical model, even if this means repeating calculations across multiple reports.

 

Example Architecture from a Real Project

entry.title: Fast-Track Your AI Journey with SAP HANA Cloud’s Vector Engine Through Our New Expert Guided service
entry.content:
Fast-Track Your AI Journey with Hands-On Experience of SAP HANA Cloud’s Vector Engine Through Our New Expert-Guided Implementation service

Are you ready to put generative AI to work using your own enterprise data - in a secure and scalable way?

SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council is piloting a new expert guided implementation service (EGI) focused on multi-model programming with SAP HANA Cloud vector engine and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). As a participant, you’ll gain exclusive early access to content, influence the final offering with your feedback, and build hands-on experience before general availability. This isn’t just another webinar - it’s a practical, step-by-step experience where you’ll configure, test, and build solutions in your own environment, with guidance from an expert throughout.

This structured approach ensures you gain both theoretical knowledge and practical experience, helping you build confidence in using SAP solutions effectively. Since SAP Enterprise Support is included with every SAP cloud subscription, you can access this offering at no additional cost. 

After the completion of the 5 day remote workshop, you will have the knowledge of multiple model programming, including scenarios using spatial and graph processing, AutoML, vector engine and document store and the knowledge to query a document store through generative AI.


What You’ll Gain
 This service will equip you with the skills to leverage:

  • Spatial and graph data processing
  • AutoML and document stores
  • RAG application that connects enterprise data to LLMs
  • Vector search in SAP HANA Cloud

Why It Matters?
With the vector engine, SAP HANA Cloud becomes the bedrock of enterprise-grade AI applications - supporting similarity search, contextual insights, and intelligent applications all from within your secure SAP landscape.

What You’ll Do?

  • Set up and develop in a HANA Cloud trial or productive account
  • Work directly with spatial, graph, and vector data models
  • Extend a real RAG scenario using your own development environment
  • Gain practical experience with SAP expert support

Who Should Join?
The service is tailor-made for:

  • Developers and Data Scientists building intelligent apps
  • Process consultants driving innovation
  • Modelers exploring new use cases in SAP HANA Cloud

Join the EGI service and gain first-hand experience with SAP’s latest innovation in multi-model programming. Learn more and register for the service by contacting SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council through esac@sap.com

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 

About the author: 

  • Robert Kelly is Architect Lead for BTP for SAP Enterprise Support. Based in Dublin, Ireland, Robert has been with SAP since 2005, holding various roles across the organization. Today, he focuses on helping businesses implement their digital transformation through SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

entry.title: HANA - Log /hana/log full situation
entry.content:
If HANA is running into a Disk Full Situation, mostly in a replication setup, it is the /hana/log volume filling up with Log Segments.

To get the actual Log Segment Situation and partial immediate help, either use:

Option A, via SQL statement:

select b.host, b.service_name, a.state, count(*) from "PUBLIC"."M_LOG_SEGMENTS" a join "PUBLIC"."M_SERVICES" b on (a.host = b.host AND a.port = b.port) group by b.host, b.service_name, a.state;

Option B, via OS command as <sid>adm user:

hdbnsutil -listServices --allDatabases --sapcontrol=1 | grep -P "service_name|process_id|volume|database_name" | awk '/^service.*service_name=/ {s=$0; next} /^service.*process_id=/ {p=$0; next} /^service.*volume=/ {split($0,v,"="); if (v[2] != 0) {print s; print p; print $0; show_dbname=1} else {show_dbname=0}; next} /^service.*database_name=/ {if (show_dbname) {print $0; print ""}}'; for i in $(find -L /hana/log/${DIR_INSTANCE:9:3} -name 'hdb*' -type d) ; do echo $i ; hdblogdiag seglist $i | grep -oP 'Formatting|Preallocated|Writing|\bClosed\b|Truncated|BackedUp|RetainedFree|\bFree\b|Unknown|ClosedIncomplete|FreeIncomplete' | sort | uniq -c ; done

A Sample of Option B:

Sample Setup:
physical Hostname: hdb-11-85static
virtual Hosntame: hana-11-virtual
SID: HSR
Instance ID: 77
hdb-11-85static:~ # su -l <sid>adm

hsradm@hdb-11-85static:/usr/sap/HSR/HDB77> hdbnsutil -listServices --allDatabases --sapcontrol=1 | grep -P "service_name|process_id|volume|database_name" | awk '/^service.*service_name=/ {s=$0; next} /^service.*process_id=/ {p=$0; next} /^service.*volume=/ {split($0,v,"="); if (v[2] != 0) {print s; print p; print $0; show_dbname=1} else {show_dbname=0}; next} /^service.*database_name=/ {if (show_dbname) {print $0; print ""}}'; for i in $(find -L /hana/log/${DIR_INSTANCE:9:3} -name 'hdb*' -type d) ; do echo $i ; hdblogdiag seglist $i | grep -oP 'Formatting|Preallocated|Writing|\bClosed\b|Truncated|BackedUp|RetainedFree|\bFree\b|Unknown|ClosedIncomplete|FreeIncomplete' | sort | uniq -c ; done

OUTPUT:

service/hana-11-virtual/37701/service_name=nameserver
service/hana-11-virtual/37701/process_id=7329
service/hana-11-virtual/37701/volume=1
service/hana-11-virtual/37701/database_name=

service/hana-11-virtual/37703/service_name=indexserver
service/hana-11-virtual/37703/process_id=26467
service/hana-11-virtual/37703/volume=3
service/hana-11-virtual/37703/database_name=HSR

service/hana-11-virtual/37707/service_name=xsengine
service/hana-11-virtual/37707/process_id=26470
service/hana-11-virtual/37707/volume=2
service/hana-11-virtual/37707/database_name=HSR

/hana/log/HSR/mnt00001/hdb00001
     21 Free
      1 Writing

/hana/log/HSR/mnt00001/hdb00003.00003
     20 Free
      1 Writing

/hana/log/HSR/mnt00001/hdb00002.00003
     22 Free
      1 RetainedFree
      1 Writing

Most of the log segments are in state Free and RetainedFree can removed via below commands:

Free
-> This is an indicator for a similar log volume full situation in the past. Although the log volume appears to be full, the free & already allocated (e.g. indexserver) log segments will be re-used.   
-> You can release the space occupied by the allocated log-segments in your file system:
#1: via SQL command: ALTER SYSTEM RECLAIM LOG;

#2: via OS command: hdbcons –p <PID_of_service> 'log release'

Via the results of the OS command in the very first step, Option B, you get back information about the PID (Process ID) and the Volume.

The Volume ID hides within the Information of "hdb0000x", x stands for the Volume ID.
The Volume Path and the PID can be used now to run the specific release command:

SAMPLE:
hsradm@hdb-11-85static:/usr/sap/HSR/HDB77> hdbcons -p 7329 'log release'

SAP HANA DB Management Client Console (type '\?' to get help for client commands)
Try to open connection to server process with PID 7329
SAP HANA DB Management Server Console (type 'help' to get help for server commands)
Executable: hdbnameserver (PID: 7329)

Service: nameserver@hana-11-virtual:37701 (DB#1: SYSTEMDB)
[OK]
--
## Start command at: 2025-05-26 11:02:15.249
Released 20 segments
[OK]
## Finish command at: 2025-05-26 11:02:15.255 command took: 5.589 msec
--
[EXIT]
--
[BYE]

 
RetainedFree

-> If the connection to the secondary is not available, the primary system will keep writing the redo log segments in the online log area to be prepared for the delta log shipping after the connection is reestablished.   

-> These log segments are marked as RetainedFree until the secondary is in sync again. , see KBA 2821539 - Volume /hana/log is full with log segments in status RetainedFree   

-> You can release the space occupied by the allocated log-segments in your file system via hdbcons by executing the OS Command as <sid>adm user: 

-> hdbcons –p <PID_of_service> 'log release -r' 

-> After deleting Log Segments "RetainedFree" the secondary will perform a full datashipping and log this message "Missing logsegments unavailable on primary - reinitialize"  

-> Check if the Parameter global.in/[system_replication]/enable_log_retention is set to force_on_takeover or force.

-> If yes then see 3102080 - RetainedFree status log segment accumulated on replication tier site while replication status is normal 

 

Configuration parameter related:   
logshipping_max_retention_size (KBA 2526877 - SAP HANA System Replication Parameter logshipping_max_retention_size) 

Log Retention and Multitarget System Replication:
SAP HANA System Replication 
SAP HANA Administration Guide for SAP HANA Platform 

 

How to Avoid Log Full Situations

To avoid log volumes running full due to non-working system replication follow the recommendations from SAP Guide:

SAP HANA Troubleshooting and Performance Analysis Guide - LogReplay: Managing the Size of the Log File at: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_HANA_PLATFORM/bed8c14f9f024763b0777aa72b5436f6/d04835a010d3485c87992619dce89e2d.html?locale=en-US

 

1) Unregister an Unused Secondary

If the secondary is disconnected for a prolonged period and if it is not to be used as a replication server anymore, then disable / unregister and decouple the secondary site and disable the primary functionality.

This will stop the RetainedFree log entries from being written.

Unregister the secondary; this is normally done from the secondary site but can be done from the primary if the secondary is not available anymore,

SAP Note 1945676 - Correct usage of hdbnsutil -sr_unregister.

hdbnsutil –sr_unregister

Disable the primary (from the primary site. In a 3 way replication Site B (secondary) is the "primary" for SITE C):

hdbnsutil –sr_disable

Execute reconfigure on the primary site:

hdbnsutil –reconfig

You can use this same procedure for a primary which, after a takeover, will no longer be used for failback.

2) Set a Maximum Retention Size

Another option to manage the log size is to set a value for the logshipping_max_retention_size parameter. If the log size reaches this limit, then RetainedFree log entries will be overwritten. Note the following points in this case:

 

  • If any RetainedFree log entries are lost, then synchronization by logreplay will no longer be possible and a full data shipment will be necessary to resynchronize the secondary.
  • It is not possible to switch back to delta mode to resynchronize - only a full data shipping is possible.

Note that the parameter logshipping_max_retention_size is set for a service. See for the details SAP KBA 0002526877 - SAP HANA System Replication Parameter logshipping_max_retention_size.

3) Placeholder File

As a general precaution, to prevent any disk full situation from arising you can reserve a portion of the disk with an emergency placeholder file (containing any dummy values), for example, occupying 5 – 10 % of the file system. This file can then be deleted if ever necessary to quickly solve disk full situations.

4) Using the HANACleaner to automatically remove LogSegments with Status FREE, ##2399996 - How-To: Configuring automatic SAP HANA Cleanup with SAP HANACleaner

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/hana-housekeeping-using-hanacleaner/ba-p/13351394

 

Log Segment Knowledge, Background Information & Sources
Data and Log Volumes Documentation: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_HANA_PLATFORM/6b94445c94ae495c83a19646e7c3fd56/be3e5310bb571014b3fbd51035bc2383.html?locale=en-US&version=2.0.02 

  

The following log segment states exist:  

FormattingThe log segment is being formatted and not yet used.
PreallocatedThe log segment has been preallocated, but never used.
WritingThe log segment is currently being written.  
ClosedThe log segment is closed, not backed up and is still required for restart.  
TruncatedThe log segment is not required for restart, but has not been backed up.  
BackedUpSegment is already backed up, but a savepoint has not yet been written. Therefore it needs to be kept for instance recovery. 
RetainedFreeThe log segment has been backed up and is not required for restart, but is required to resync the system replication sites.  
FreeThe log segment has been backed up, it is not required for restart and can be reused.  

  

Per default each service can store up to 10240 log segments. 
If this number is reached, the log reports a log full error even if there is still disk space available.  
This limit can be changed: 2072410 - Enlarge limitation of log segment number of LogSegment Directory 
The new value of log segments that can be allocated can be checked via SQL (only) by connecting to each individual service: SELECT HOST,PORT,VOLUME_ID,PARTITION_ID,PATH,TOTAL_SEGMENTS FROM M_LOG_PARTITIONS  

  

SAP NOTES / KBAs Log related:  
Temporary Workaround: 1679938 - DiskFullEvent on Log Volume
2409471 - SAP HANA Log Volume Full on Secondary or Tertiary Site with System Replication 
2821539 - Volume /hana/log is full with log segments in status RetainedFree 
2781933 - Nameserver Crash at DataRecovery::LogSegment::freeRetainedSegment When Executing Command hdbcons log release -r
2988616 - /hana/shared volume full or growing
3047379 - Monitoring hana/log volume consumption
3142505 - Limit the Maximum Size Used by HANA Log Segments
SAMPLE: ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('global.ini', 'SYSTEM' ) SET ('persistence', 'log_disk_usage_limit') = '85' WITH RECONFIGURE COMMENT SAP Note 3142505. Log volume usage size limited to 85%';  

2083715 - Analyzing log volume full situations
1679938 - DiskFullEvent on Log Volume
3537839 - SAP HANA Database Startup Issue Due to Insufficient Disk Space

entry.title: Authorization group
entry.content:
How They Work

When a user attempts to access an SAP object, the system checks two things:

1️⃣Whether the user has the necessary authorization (through roles and profiles)
2️⃣Whether the user is authorized for the specific authorization group assigned to that object

If an object belongs to an authorization group that the user cannot access, the system will deny access even if the user has other relevant permissions.

Common Use Cases

Table Protection: Database tables containing sensitive information (like payroll data, financial records, or personal information) are assigned to specific authorization groups. Only users with appropriate clearance can access these tables through transactions like SE16 or development tools.

Program Security: Custom programs or reports can be assigned authorization groups to ensure only designated users can execute them, particularly useful for programs that manipulate critical business data.

Configuration and Assignment

Authorization groups are typically defined in customization tables and then assigned to objects through various methods depending on the object type. Users are granted access to authorization groups through their roles, which contain authorization objects (like S_TABU_DIS for table access) that specify which authorization groups they can access.

Demo

Create new authorization group in the SM30, view name V_TPGP.

Add this group to the program (see explanation above). Now if the user without appropriate clearance try to execute the report the error message will be appears.

Add the group to the related field of the object.

The role should be assigned to the user.

Now the program can be executed

entry.title: Enhanced Organizational Structure Profile in SAP Readiness Check for SAP ERP Usage & Data Profiling
entry.content:
Organizations planning a transition to SAP S/4HANA require comprehensive tools that offer insights into their existing SAP ERP Central Component (SAP ECC) systems. The Organizational Structure Profile within SAP Readiness Check for SAP ERP Usage and Data Profiling serves this purpose. Today, we proudly announce the availability of the new version of the Organizational Structure Profile as part of SAP Readiness Check for SAP ERP Usage and Data Profiling. We deliver significantly enhanced analysis features in this latest version, providing better clarity, deeper analytical capabilities, and a more user-friendly interface.

Figure 1: Updated Version of the Organizational Structure Profile

Understanding the Business Context

The Organizational Structure Profile provides a holistic overview of an organization's SAP ECC 6.0 system structure. This profile is essential for understanding the relationships between various organizational units and identifying opportunities for optimization before transitioning to SAP S/4HANA. The Organizational Structure Profile automates a previously manual and resource-intensive process, enabling organizations to reduce the time and cost associated with scoping and assessing organizational objects.

The enhancements in this new version address several shortcomings of the previous version, including:

  • Limited scope: The previous version lacked coverage for certain organizational items (e.., controlling areas, storage locations, distribution channels, etc.), preventing a comprehensive analysis.
  • Lack of relationship data: The absence of explicit relationship data hindered a complete understanding of organizational dynamics.
  • Simplistic user interface: Users were unable to efficiently access and interact with detailed and interconnected information due to a flat and feature-limited user interface.

The latest version addresses these shortcomings, transforming the Organizational Structure Profile into a more robust and insightful tool.

Key Capabilities of the Enhanced Organizational Structure Profile

Expanded Scope and Coverage of Organizational Item Types

The updated version significantly broadens its scope by including additional organizational item types essential for a comprehensive understanding of the organizational structure. These include:

  • Controlling Areas
  • Cost Centers
  • Profit Centers
  • Company Codes
  • Plants
  • Shipping Points
  • Storage Locations
  • Purchasing Organizations
  • Sales Organizations
  • Distribution Channels
  • Divisions

This expanded coverage gives organizations a holistic view of their structure, ensuring that all relevant organizational elements are considered and more thoroughly examined during the transition. This insight allows project teams to make informed decisions during the transition planning phase.

Enhanced Visualization of Organizational Relationships

This new version offers improved visualization capabilities to clarify the complex relationships between organizational items. These include:

  • Hierarchical network graphs: Graphical representations display parent (ancestor) and child relationships, clearly visualizing organizational hierarchies.
  • Color-coded structures: Different organizational types are represented with distinct colors, improving the clarity and usability of network visualizations.
  • Expandable/collapsible elements: Network components can expand and collapse, allowing users to focus on specific areas of interest within the organizational structure.

Figure 2: The Parent Object and Child Object Network Graphs of a Sales Organization Item

These advanced visualization capabilities facilitate a more intuitive understanding of organizational structures and interdependencies, empowering project teams to make well-informed decisions during transition planning.

Detailed Usage Analysis for Making Informed Decisions

The enhancements include incorporating detailed usage analysis to help organizations quickly identify actively used organizational items and those that may be obsolete. Historically, determining whether an organizational unit is actively contributing to business processes or is no longer relevant is time-consuming. This new version now simplifies this process with advanced usage indicators, enabling teams to streamline assessments, accelerate decision-making based on usage data, and optimize resources by eliminating obsolete elements.

To evaluate the usage status of an organizational item, related business objects for all organizational item types in scope were identified and are used to analyze their data volumes and activities regarding the selected organization. The resulting data for these business objects are presented in the "Usage Indicator" card of the detail view (see figure 3). Each business object is assigned a usage status, categorized as "Active," "Likely Not Active," or "Not Active":

  • Active (Green): Indicates that the business object is currently actively used for the organizational item in focus, having a data volume higher than 20 items and recent activity over the last three years.
  • Likely Not Active (Yellow): States that the usage status of this business object is uncertain about the selected organizational item, with limited data volumes or lack of recent activity, warranting further investigation.
  • Not Active (Red): Confirms that the business object is not being used for the selected organizational item, with a data volume less than 20 items and no recent activity over the last three years.

Based on the usage indicators of the related business objects, the usage status of the selected organizational item is determined and presented in the header of the detail view (see figure 3):

  • Active (Green): The organizational item is currently actively used. It has at least one related business object which is actively used.
  • Likely Not Active (Yellow): The usage status of the selected organizational item is ambiguous, demanding further investigation. No related business object is actively used, but there are objects with usage status "Likely Not Active" and/or "Not Active."
  • Not Active (Red): The organizational item is not being used. All related business objects have the usage indicator value "Not Active."

For a more detailed understanding of calculating usage indicators, click on the 'Learn More' button to access the Organizational Structure Profile help panel.

Figure 3: Detail View of a Plant Organizational Item, Including Usage Indicators Card and Open Help Panel

We strongly recommend that the project team, particularly the business owners of the involved organizational units, conduct further investigations to validate and confirm the findings from the usage data analysis. This additional validation ensures the accuracy and reliability of the insights, facilitating more informed decision-making.

Identification of Orphaned Organizational Items

The enhanced version automatically identifies, and flags orphaned organizational items that lack proper parent-child hierarchy links, signaling potential structural inefficiencies or errors. By highlighting these gaps, the Organizational Structure Profile enables organizations to address structural inconsistencies proactively, minimize transition risks, and ensure the integrity and completeness of their organizational model throughout the transition process.

Figure 4: A Plant Without a Corresponding Company Code is Marked as an Orphaned Item

 

Redesigned User Interface for Enhanced Usability

To address usability concerns, the enhanced Organizational Structure Profile features a completely redesigned user interface that significantly improves usability:

  • Flexible column layout: The interface is divided into two main sections - a list of organizational items on the left and detailed information about the selected item on the right.
  • Intuitive filtering: Users can filter organizational items by type through a view selector or search for specific items using a text-based search field.
  • Detailed information sections: The detail view includes:
    • Master data information
    • Usage indicators showing the status of related business objects
    • Dependent data sections displaying related organizational items
    • Network graphs visualizing parent and child hierarchies
  • Export capabilities: Users can export both the list of organizational items and detailed information about specific items for further analysis or reporting.
  • Related data access: Links to related data collectors enable users to access additional information relevant to the selected organizational item type.

Figure 5: Detail View of a Company Code Organizational Item and Available Filter Options

This newly designed user interface makes it easier for project teams to navigate and analyze complex organizational structures without requiring specialized knowledge.

Cross-Profile Integration

The enhanced Organizational Structure Profile integrates seamlessly with other profiles within the SAP Readiness Check for SAP ERP Usage and Data Profiling:

  • Usage indicators are linked to the Initial Data Migration Profile, allowing users to navigate directly to detailed information about related business objects. This integration provides a more holistic view of the system landscape and facilitates comprehensive analysis.
  • Hyperlinks to related data collectors enable users to access additional information and gain a more comprehensive understanding of their system landscape.

Figure 6: A Company Code Organizational Item and its Collection of Links to Related Data Collectors

Use Cases

Transitioning to SAP S/4HANA can be more than just a data migration exercise - it could be a strategic opportunity to optimize business structures and eliminate inefficiencies. The enhanced Organizational Structure Profile is a powerful tool that offers valuable insights regardless of the transition scenario.

Selective Data Transition Planning

Understanding the organizational structure and usage patterns is crucial for projects involving selective data transition. The new version helps identify actively used organizational elements, highlighting those that should be migrated versus those that can be discarded, optimizing the transition process.

Organizational Structure Optimization

Many organizations use the transition to SAP S/4HANA as an opportunity to streamline their organizational structure. The enhanced analysis provides the insight needed to identify redundant or unused organizational elements, supporting informed decision-makingduring this optimization process.

Transition Sequence Planning

Understanding dependencies between organizational items is essential for planning the transition sequence. The network visualization capabilities of the Organizational Structure Profile make these dependencies clear, helping project teams develop more effective transition strategies.

Data Quality Assessment

Orphaned item detection and usage analysis contribute to an overall data quality assessment. This evaluation enables organizations to address inconsistencies and ensure that only relevant, high-quality data migrates to the new system.

Next Steps for Users

Follow these steps to take advantage of the new Organizational Structure Profile:

  1. System preparation and data collection: Implement the latest version of SAP Note 3112362 and run an SAP Readiness Check for SAP ERP Usage and Data Profiling analysis by following the instructions described within the SAP Note, including uploading the data collection archive to the SAP Readiness Check cloud application.
  2. Explore the analysis results: Identify organizational items of interest and examine their usage and dependent data.
  3. Visualize hierarchical relationships: Use the network graphs to understand the relationships between specific organizational items.
  4. Filter and search: Use the filtering and search capabilities to focus on specific segments of the organizational structure.
  5. Identify optimization opportunities: Review usage status indicators to identify potentially unused or redundant organizational items.
  6. Investigate orphaned items: Address organizational items with disrupted hierarchy paths to ensure structural consistency.
  7. Explore related data: Use the links to associated data collectors to gain additional insights about specific organizational item types.
  8. Export for collaboration: Export relevant data for sharing with stakeholders and for further analysis.

As an alternative to creating a new analysis in the SAP Readiness Check cloud application, you can update your existing SAP Readiness Check for SAP ERP Usage and Data Profiling analysis. Start by implementing the latest version of SAP Note 3112362 and rerun the data collection process in your SAP ECC system. Then, using the newly created data collection archive, update the existing analysis data using the Update Analysis option within the header of the dashboard view in the SAP Readiness Check cloud application on SAP for Me or your SAP Cloud ALM tenant.

 As always, we look forward to receiving your feedback.

SAP Readiness Check team

entry.title: Join the SAP S/4HANA Service, Private Cloud Edition Early Adopter Care Program
entry.content:
Are you running classic Customer Service (CS) within the Compatibility Scope in S/4HANA*, and wondering how to transition to SAP S/4HANA Service?

You’re not alone. Many organizations are asking the same questions: Where do we start? What’s the benefit? Who can guide us?

We understand the challenges, and we’re here to support your transformation journey.

* Note: The Compatibility Scope for CS is set to expire at the end of 2030 (unlike most cases, which expire at the end of 2025). This makes it essential to begin planning your transition. For more details, refer to SAP Note 2269324 and this blog post.

Why join this program?

The SAP S/4HANA Service, Private Cloud Edition Early Adopter Care Program is your opportunity to engage directly with SAP’s product development teams. Our mission is simple: to refine and evolve our solutions based on real customer needs. Your voice matters.

As a participant, you’ll gain direct access to SAP product experts who can guide you through your transition from CS to S/4HANA Service. You’ll receive support with product-related questions and benefit from close monitoring of your critical issues. Most importantly, you’ll have the opportunity to share your feedback and help shape the future of SAP Service – at no cost to you.

Who should join?

This program is designed for customers who are licensed for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and are running on SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS0 or higher. If you're still using classic CS in the Compatibility Scope within SAP S/4HANA and have concrete plans for a proof of concept or implementation project, this is your chance to get expert guidance. We’re looking for participants who are ready to engage regularly, share their experiences, and become reference customers.

To make the most of the program, you will need a dedicated project team and a well-defined internal setup to drive the implementation.

How to apply

Apply now via the SAP Influence Portal and become part of a select group helping shape the future of SAP service management capabilities. Let’s build the future of SAP S/4HANA Service together.

Join the SAP S/4HANA Service, Private Cloud Edition Early Adopter Care Program and take the next step in your transformation journey.

If you have any questions, feel free to contact us: SAP_EAC_S4H_Service@sap.com.

Download a copy of the one-pager (image below).

Learn more about SAP S/4HANA Service

  • SAP S/4HANA Service | SAP Community – collection of webinars, blog articles, links to certification program and other materials. It’s a great entry point to see all resources on SAP S/4HANA Service.
  • Recap of SAP S/4HANA 2023 Highlights Webinar: Service – 30-min webinar I conducted. I show some concepts in diagrams and show system demonstrations of highlights in the 2023 release (FPS0) for SAP S/4HANA Service.

@o_frick @Holk_H

entry.title: Re: Facing an issue while executing the Actions Artifacts...
entry.content:
Hello,

Due to some missing information, I can unfortunately only guess what went wrong when posting budget data via API API_FINPLANNINGDATA.

Posting budget data to ACDOCP is subject to additional requirements. For example project budget (planning category BUDGET01)  may only be uploaded as a whole and must therefore include the budget for the entire project. As a consequence, the delete scope must NOT be set for PS_POSID (Work Breakdown Structure Element), POPER/FISCPER and RBUKRS (Company Code), otherwise only a partial update of the project budget would be made (that is not permitted).

Similar limitations apply for cost center budget (planning category BUDGET02) that may only be uploaded as a whole and must therefore include the budget for the entire year. In order to ensure a complete cost center budget, the delete scope must NOT be set for the FISCPER or POPER.

Can you please revise your payload according to the above information and try again to post budget data?

If the problems still occurs, for further analyses I need the payload and the complete response from your post request.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Processes can now be scheduled 📅!
entry.content:
First, when you say configured in the same agent, that means you are talking of automations, not processes. Can you clarify?

As for processes, a specific process cannot be both scheduled and triggered by API. You could have a process triggered in API and then create another process that wraps the first that is scheduled.

When the process gets triggered by API or scheduler, that creates a process instance and that instance cannot be triggered/retriggered (only processes get triggered, creating a process instance). That instance will run and will not affect any other instances (except as they update the same backend).

As for a process that has automation, when the automation is reached in the process, it will be "triggered" and run, assuming the agent tenant/mode is set properly.

P.S.: I'm hope this helped, but I feel I may not have answered your questions. Please clarify the questions if you need additional help.

entry.title: SuccessFactors - Variable Pay Basis Calculation
entry.content:
Hi All,

Good day,
I'm trying to look for the system feasibility regarding the requirement I received from my customer. You may refer to the pictures to make two scenarios below more visualize

Business considered 1st of January to 31st December 20XX as their bonus fiscal/calendar year

Scenario 1 is basically a scenario in which the employee does not get a promotion and that's why the bonus calculation should only consider the salary based on the merit Review in April (a specific as of date), excluding the salary from 1st January until March (1000 USD)

But once an employee receives a promotion scenario 2 shall take place. What does this mean:

In case of promotion the bonus calculation should consider the salary (1100 USD) based of Merit Review (in April) and the salary (1300 USD) based of Promotion (in August), excluding the salary from 1st January until March (1000 USD)
